story.title: Dr. Vega's Space Mission: The Cold Coffee Connection
story.text:
My name is Dr. Isabel Vega. I am a thermal engineer at NASA. My job is to protect astronauts and spacecraft from the extreme temperatures in space. I study how heat moves. This is more common than you think. You can see it in your own kitchen.

Imagine a hot cup of coffee. The coffee is hot, and the room is cool. This creates a temperature difference. Heat always flows from a hot object to a cooler one. At first, the temperature gap is very large. This makes the transfer rate very fast. Your coffee cools down quickly.

As the coffee cools, the temperature gap gets smaller. The heat transfer slows down. This pattern is called exponential decay. It is fast at first and then gets slower and slower. This is Newton's Law of Cooling.

Finally, the coffee’s temperature matches the room’s temperature. It reaches thermal equilibrium. Now, the net heat transfer stops. The coffee will not get any colder than the room. We use these same rules to keep our spacecraft safe on every mission.
think_abouts: Why does the coffee cool fast at the beginning?
What happens when the coffee and the room are the same temperature?
story.go_deeper: If Dr. Vega wanted to keep a satellite very cool, should she paint it a dark color or a shiny, light color? Why?
check_prompts: What makes the transfer rate of heat slow down over time?

vocabulary.definition: The state when two objects reach the same temperature and heat stops flowing between them.
vocabulary.use_sentence: My soup and the room reached thermal equilibrium, so the soup was no longer hot.

vocabulary.definition: The gap in temperature between a hot object and its cooler surroundings.
vocabulary.use_sentence: The large temperature difference between the freezer and the kitchen made the ice melt fast.

vocabulary.definition: The speed at which heat moves from a warm place to a cool place.
vocabulary.use_sentence: A metal cup has a high transfer rate, so it feels cold to touch very quickly.

vocabulary.definition: A pattern of decrease that is fast at first and then slows down over time.
vocabulary.use_sentence: The cooling of the pizza followed a curve of exponential decay.

vocabulary.definition: The scientific rule that says the rate of cooling depends on the temperature difference.
vocabulary.use_sentence: Because of Newton's Law of Cooling, the very hot tea cooled faster than the warm tea.

discussion_cards.prompt: Dr. Vega's job is to stop heat from transferring to astronauts. What are some things you use in your life to stop heat transfer (like an oven mitt or a lunch box)?
discussion_cards.frame: To stop heat transfer, I use a ___ which works by ___.

discussion_cards.prompt: If you pour hot coffee into a very warm room versus a very cold room, in which room will the coffee cool faster? Explain why using 'temperature difference'.
discussion_cards.frame: The coffee will cool faster in the ___ room because the temperature difference is ___.

discussion_cards.prompt: The story describes cooling as 'fast at first and then slower'. What is another example of something that changes fast at first and then slows down?
discussion_cards.frame: Another example of something that slows down over time is ___ because ___.

reflection_frames.knew: Before reading, I already knew that ___
reflection_frames.learned: From the story, I learned that ___
